---
title: "Code Styling Demo"
subtitle: "Inline and block code with the brand's monospace styling"
---

This page demonstrates how the `monospace`, `monospace-inline`, and
`monospace-block` typography settings from `_brand.yml` render in practice.

## Inline code

You can refer to functions like `mean()`, `dplyr::filter()`, and
`ggplot2::aes()` inline. Variable names such as `df`, `n_obs`, and the
`TRUE`/`FALSE` literals also pick up the inline monospace styling, with the
deep purple color.

A typical sentence might note that we call `summary()` on a `data.frame` to get
a quick overview, or use `<-` to assign a value.

## Block code

Here is a code block computing summary statistics:

```r
library(dplyr)

# `penguins` ships with base R (the datasets package) as of R 4.5.0
penguins |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    mean_mass  = mean(body_mass, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_mass    = sd(body_mass, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

And a block that fits a simple linear model and plots it:

```r
library(ggplot2)

model <- lm(body_mass ~ flipper_len, data = penguins)
summary(model)

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_len, y = body_mass)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass vs. flipper length",
    x     = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y     = "Body mass (g)"
  )
```

Block code uses the `monospace-block` background, while short references like
`geom_smooth()` stay inline.
